data.title: BP Target Cut to 400p From 525p by UBS
Market Chatter: ASEAN Ministers to Not Retaliate Against US Tariffs
data.description:
ASEAN economic ministers agreed not to retaliate against recent US tariffs and instead called for open dialogue to resolve trade tensions, Taipei Times reported Friday.

According to the joint statement, the report noted that retaliation would harm the region, as the US remains ASEAN's largest investor and second-biggest trading partner. The ministers expressed concern over the tariffs but emphasized the importance of maintaining a stable economic relationship.

Vietnam and Cambodia were hit with the highest duties, while others like Malaysia and the Philippines faced lower rates. The meeting was chaired by Malaysia, which holds the rotating ASEAN chair. Meanwhile, the EU also paused its planned tariffs, following a 90-day suspension by the US, the publication said.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: West River Eyes MYR42 Million IPO
data.description:
West River (KLSE:WESTRVR) is targeting to raise 41.9 million ringgit via its initial public offering on the Ace Market of Bursa Malaysia.

The IPO of the mechanical and electrical (M&E) engineering services provider involves a public issue of 71.5 million shares and an offer for sale of 35.8 million shares at 0.39 ringgit apiece, according to a Thursday Malaysian bourse filing.

Proceeds will be used for expansion and establishment of fiber optic communication networks infrastructure, repayment of bank borrowings, and capital expenditure, it said.

data.title: French Stocks Extend Gains, Still Set for Weekly Loss
data.description:
The CAC 40 rose 0.8% to 7,184 on Friday, marking its second consecutive session of gains, as investor sentiment improved following the suspension of EU and US tariffs.

The European Union announced a temporary 90-day pause on its planned retaliatory tariffs against the US, mirroring Washington's own reprieve and signaling a willingness on both sides to return to the negotiating table.

However, caution remained as the US pressed ahead with tariff hikes on Chinese imports, a move that rekindled fears of a prolonged trade war and continued to cast a shadow over global growth prospects.

Most stocks traded higher, with STMicroelectronics leading the index with a 2.6% gain, followed by Kering, whose shares rose 2.2%, and Pernod Ricard, with shares up 1.7%.

On the downside, Stellantis NV was the worst performer among the few decliners, with shares falling 2.1%.

Despite Friday’s gains, the index remains on track to post a weekly loss.

data.title: Croatia's TSH plans 132 euro per share dividend for 2024
data.description:
ZAGREB (Croatia), April 11 (SeeNews) - Croatian fodder producer Tvornica Stocne Hrane (TSH) [ZSE:TSHC] said it will propose to its shareholders to distribute a dividend of 132 euro ($149.60) per share for 2024.

The dividend of 1.8 million euro in total will be paid out from the company's profit retained in the period before the end of 2000 and from 2001 to 2004, it said in an invitation to an annual shareholders' meeting, to be held on May 6.

The company reported a net profit of 3.9 million euro for last year. TSH will propose to its shareholders to allocate 0.5% of the 2024 net profit to statutory reserves and to retain the remainder.

Record date for the dividend payout is May 9, ex date is May 8 and payment day will be May 13.

Last year it paid a divided of 120 euro per share.

Shares of TSH last traded on March 25, ending 6.21% lower at 2,720 euro at the Progress trading platform for less liquid small and medium sized-companies run by the Zagreb bourse.

($ = 0.882 euro)

data.title: Concord Enviro Sets Up Step-Down Subsidiary in Kenya
data.description:
Concord Enviro Systems said its wholly owned subsidiary, Concord Enviro (FZE), has incorporated a new step-down subsidiary in Kenya named Concord Enviro Africa to provide industrial water and wastewater treatment solutions.

The new entity has been formed with a cash subscription of 100% share capital amounting to 100,000 Kenyan Shillings, according to a Thursday filing.

data.title: US 10-Year Treasury Yield Posts Steep Weekly Rise
data.description:
The yield on the US 10-year Treasury note approached 4.5% before settling around 4.4% on Friday and is on track to post a nearly 43bps increase for the week, signaling Treasuries may be losing their safe-haven appeal.

This week’s bond market sell-off reflected a broader retreat by foreign investors from US assets, triggering a sharp decline in equities, the dollar, and government bonds amid growing concerns over the economic fallout from President Trump's trade policies.

Tensions with China have escalated, with tariffs on Chinese goods now reaching as high as 145%.

Meanwhile, the US continues to impose a 10% tariff on imports for most countries, along with a 25% levy on steel, aluminum, and autos.

While the 90-day truce announced by Trump offered a brief reprieve and raised hopes for resumed trade negotiations, investor sentiment remains fragile.

Fears of a potential stagflation scenario are gaining traction.

data.title: Aeon: to Acquire 7.2% Stake in Tsuruha from Nomura Securities
Italian Shares Underperform
data.description:
The FTSE MIB fell to around 34,261 in early trading on Friday, trimming a nearly 5% gain from the previous session.

The index underperformed its regional peers and is heading for its second consecutive weekly loss, with investors closely monitoring the escalating US-China trade war and its potential economic fallout.

Meanwhile, the European Union has temporarily paused its planned retaliatory tariffs for the same 90-day period, aligning with Washington’s move to foster dialogue.

Among individual stocks, top laggards included Stellantis (-1.6%), Assicurazioni Generali (-0.7%), Ferrari (-0.6%), and Prymian (-0.6%).

Offsetting some of the losses were notable gains from Enel (+1%), STMicroelectronics (+2.6%), Moncler (+1.2%), and Davide Campari-Milano (+2.7%).

data.title: UK gilt yields rise after faster-than-expected GDP growth
data.description:
British government bond yields rose on Friday after stronger-than-expected growth data somewhat curbed expectations for Bank of England rate cuts, though focus remained on the market implications of U.S. tariff policy.

Britain's economy expanded 0.5% in February , the fastest gross domestic product growth in 11 months and well above the 0.1% expected in a Reuters poll.

Interest rate-sensitive two-year gilt yields (GB2YT=RR) rose 5 basis points on the day to 3.96% at 0740 GMT versus broadly flat U.S. Treasuries (US2YT=RR) and German bonds (EU2YT=RR).

Ten-year gilt yields (GB10YT=RR) were also 5 bps higher at 4.697% but this was broadly in line with U.S. and German moves.

Investors on Friday were pricing in 82 bps of further Bank of England rate cuts this year, down from more than 90 bps on Thursday but above the roughly 50 bps expected before U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff announcements last week.

Thirty-year gilt yields (GB30YT=RR) - which tumbled 16.8 bps on Thursday, their biggest daily drop in over two years - were more than 7 bps higher at 5.502%, resuming the upward climb seen since Trump's tariffs roiled markets.

Thirty-year gilt yields hit their highest since 1998 on Wednesday with their biggest one-day rise since October 2022, which came in the wake of former Prime Minister Liz Truss' poorly received budget plans.

"The acute volatility is akin to that seen at only the most extreme of circumstances," Barclays fixed income strategist Moyeen Islam said about the week's market moves.

data.title: German finance minister: EU is ready to respond if trade talks with U.S. unsuccessful
data.description:
The European Union will be ready to respond to U.S. tariffs if negotiations with the United States do not work, German Finance Minister Joerg Kukies said on Friday.

"The U.S. side has to be aware that if negotiations do not work, then we will have another discussion around response mechanisms," Kukies said in Warsaw ahead of a meeting of EU finance ministers.

The attention of global markets has returned to U.S. President Donald Trump's escalating trade war with China and recession fears, following a brief reprieve after Trump decided to pause duties earlier this week.

Kukies also warned that Trump's decision to pause trade tariffs for dozens of countries for 90 days may add to uncertainty and keep markets and company executives on edge.

"What remains is the uncertainty, which no CEO likes," the outgoing finance minister said in an interview in German newspaper Handelsblatt's Friday edition.

"It potentially increases uncertainty even more. After all, there could be further escalation after 90 days," he added.

The minister noted that the European Commission has been diligent to calibrate its potential responses in sectors where the European industry has viable alternatives, while keeping consumer segments in mind that rely on U.S. providers.

"Digital retailer measures are not great because there are no real alternatives to the services which Americans provide," Kukies said, calling for increasing the competitiveness of the EU digital sector.

data.title: China warns Hong Kong's last major opposition party to disband, members say
data.description:
Key points:
  • Democratic Party told it should be 'gone' before Dec election
  • 5 democrats say pressure from Beijing reason for disbanding
  • Diplomats say shutdown will further dent HK's reputation
  • Democratic Party to hold meeting on April 13 on next steps

Five senior members of Hong Kong's Democratic Party, the city's biggest and last remaining major opposition party, say Chinese officials or middlemen have warned the parties to disband or face serious consequences, including possible arrests.

The Democratic Party, which was founded in 1994 in the years before Hong Kong's historic return from British to Chinese rule, has over the past few decades been the flagship opposition party that has united the city's democratic forces to push Beijing on democratic reforms and to uphold the financial hub's freedoms.

Amid a years-long national security crackdown by China after pro-democracy protests in 2019, the Democratic Party will hold an extraordinary general meeting on April 13 to seek members' views and possibly pave the way for the group's dissolution.

The group's chairman, Lo Kin-hei, has not given a concrete reason for the likely disbandment, but five senior Democratic Party members told Reuters they had been told in meetings with Chinese officials or individuals linked to Beijing in recent months that the party should close.

Fred Li, a veteran democratic party member and former lawmaker, said a Chinese official had told him this should be done before this December's legislative elections.

"The meaning is that we should be gone by then," Li told Reuters. "The message was very direct."

Li declined to identify the individual but said the tone was very different from frequent exchanges he has had with Chinese officials over many years.

There were no immediate responses to requests for comment from the Hong Kong Liaison Office, China's main representative body in Hong Kong, or from the Hong Kong government.

Four other senior Democratic Party members also said they had been warned in recent months by middlemen linked to Beijing, some of whom warned the party would face "serious consequences" if it didn't disband. Three declined to be identified given the sensitivity of the matter.

Yeung Sum, one of the founding members of the Democratic Party, said Beijing's move in 2021 to overhaul the city's electoral system to only allow "patriots" to run for public office had effectively marginalised the party by removing it from mainstream politics.

The party now holds no seats in Hong Kong's legislature.

"We just keep a voice of advocacy for the people of Hong Kong on social and political issues, but still we are under pressure," Yeung, who was also approached by a middleman, told Reuters.

Two Asian and two Western diplomats say they are aware of the veiled threats to the Democratic Party.

"For a long time it seemed like Beijing could live with the situation of having the party around as a figment of opposition," said one Western envoy.

"It seems they are leaving nothing to chance. The message is it is time to close down once and for all," said the diplomat, who was not authorised to speak publicly.

The envoys see the demise of the Democratic Party as further denting Hong Kong's international reputation amid U.S.-China geopolitical tensions. China's recent criticism of CK Hutchison's port sale to a U.S. consortium has also raised questions about the city’s autonomy, they say.

Founded in 1994 and led by barrister Martin Lee, the democratic Party has played a pivotal role in Hong Kong's transition from British to Chinese rule, shaping the financial hub's democratic development and civil society.

Emily Lau, a former Democratic Party chairwoman, said she was saddened by the likely dissolution, as she had always believed in engagement rather than confrontation with Beijing, including a 2013 meeting with the deputy head of the Liaison Office, Li Gang.

"What we want is to see a safe, just and ultimately free Hong Kong," she said. Lau declined to comment on whether she'd been approached about disbanding.

If the democrats disband, it would mark the end of nearly 30 years of opposition party politics in Hong Kong.

At least five democratic party members are currently in jail or held in custody under the national security law.

China says the security laws have brought stability to Hong Kong and rejects claims by some countries such as the United States that it has been used as a tool of repression against the democrats.

"Hong Kong's international reputation was built on its openness, its freedoms, and its respect for the rule of law," said David Alton, a life peer of Britain's House of Lords and patron of Hong Kong Watch, a rights advocacy group.

"The disbanding of the Democratic Party is another sign that Hong Kong is now being subjected to the same censorship and repression already familiar in mainland China."

data.title: Longyun Cultural Creation & Tech to Build Film Base for 1 Billion Yuan
data.description:
Shanghai Longyun Cultural Creation & Technology Group plans to build the Tianzhushan Film and Cultural Tourism Base in Qianshan City, Anhui Province, China for 1 billion yuan, according to a Shanghai Stock Exchange disclosure on Thursday.

The Chinese advertising services provider will collaborate with Anhui Tianzhushan Cultural Tourism Holding Group for the construction of the film base. Tianzhushan Cultural Tourism will manage the construction and investment, while Longyun will operate the base for 20 years.

The construction will run for 18 months, with the first phase to begin before September. Revenue will be earned beginning after March 2027.

data.title: Hong Kong's rocky democratic road under Chinese rule
data.description:
Hong Kong's main opposition Democratic Party is considering disbanding amid a years-long national security crackdown by China after mass pro-democracy protests in 2019.

Following is a timeline of key events in the democratic development of Hong Kong before and after the financial hub's handover from Britain to China in 1997.

Dec. 1984 - China and Britain sign a Joint Declaration on how Hong Kong will be governed, including a "One Country, Two Systems" formula granting a high degree of autonomy under Chinese Communist Party rule.

June 1989 - Beijing's Tiananmen Square crackdown prompts millions in Hong Kong to take to the streets to call for more democratic safeguards.

April 1990 - Beijing ratifies Hong Kong's Basic Law as a mini-constitution promising "universal suffrage" as an ultimate aim.

1994 - The Democratic Party is founded through the merger of two opposition groups: United Democrats of HK and Meeting Point.

Sept. 1995 - The Democratic Party wins 42% of the popular vote in legislative council elections, becoming the largest party during the final years of British colonial rule.

July 1997 - Hong Kong returns to Chinese sovereignty.

July 2003 - Half a million people protest against a proposed national security bill, also known as Article 23, causing it to be scrapped.

Aug. 2014 - China's parliament rules out a fully democratic election for Hong Kong's leader in 2017, by imposing tight rules on nominations of candidates who want to run.

Sept. 2014 - The civil disobedience movement known as "Occupy Central" draws hundreds of thousands of protesters who block and set up encampments in three districts including a highway outside government headquarters for 79 days to demand China allow full democracy.

2019

April 3 – Hong Kong introduces a proposed extradition bill to allow criminal suspects to be sent to China for trial.

June 12 – Police fire non-lethal rounds and tear gas to disperse hundreds of thousands of protesters calling for the extradition bill to be scrapped, marking the beginning of months long protests that turn increasingly violent.

Nov. 24 - Hong Kong's pro-democracy candidates win a landslide victory in district council elections, securing 90% of 452 seats in what is seen as a symbolic vote against China.

2020

June 30 - China's parliament imposes a sweeping national security law, drawing condemnation from the U.S. amid concerns it will be used to crack down on opposition democrats.

July 11-12 - More than 600,000 people cast ballots in an unofficial primary election to select the strongest pro-democracy candidates to contest an upcoming election.

July 30 - Hong Kong disqualifies a dozen pro-democracy candidates from running in the Sept. election, citing reasons including collusion with foreign forces.

July 31 - The September election is postponed on COVID grounds.

Nov. 11 - Hong Kong expels four democrats from the legislature.

Nov. 12 - The remaining 15 pro-democracy lawmakers resign from the 70-seat Legislative Council in protest.

2021

Jan 6 - More than 50 pro-democracy campaigners, including pro-democracy lawmakers, are arrested in the biggest crackdown to date under the national security law.

March 11 - China's parliament overhauls Hong Kong's electoral system, effectively shutting out the opposition from future elections, which some countries call an erosion of democracy in the financial hub.

Dec 19 - The turnout for an overhauled, "patriots"-only legislative election hits a record low of 30.2%.

2023

May 27 - Hong Kong's second-largest opposition group, the Civic Party, said it would disband after 30 of 31 members voted to wind up the party.

July 3 - Hong Kong police issue arrest warrants and offer bounties of HK$1 million against eight overseas-based activists.

July 6 - Hong Kong's legislature unanimously voted to overhaul district-level elections by drastically reducing the number of directly elected seats.

Dec 10 - A "patriots only" district council election, with no participation of opposition democrats, had a record low voter turnout as many spurned what was seen as an undemocratic poll.

2024

March 19 - Hong Kong lawmakers unanimously pass a new national security bill, also known as Article 23 that punishes offences including treason, sabotage, sedition, the theft of state secrets, external interference and espionage.

Nov 19 - Hong Kong's High Court sentences 45 pro-democracy activists to up to 10 years in prison for conspiracy to commit subversion over the 2020 "primary election".

Dec 24 - Hong Kong offered bounties of HK$1 million for six more pro-democracy campaigners deemed to have violated national security laws, and revoked the passports of seven more.

2025

Feb 20 - Hong Kong's Democratic Party said it would start preparations to disband and wind up its affairs after a meeting of its leadership.

data.title: HBM Plans Up to HK$200 Million Share Buyback; Shares Surge 12%
data.description:
HBM plans to repurchase up to HK$200 million of shares under a buyback mandate, according to a Thursday filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

The drug company has so far allocated HK$240 million for share buybacks in 2025 on a year-to-date basis, the filing said.

Shares surged 12% during Friday's afternoon trading.

data.title: Siemens Spain Executive Killed in Helicopter Crash in US
data.description:
Siemens' Spanish division's executive Agustín Escobar, and his family, died in a helicopter crash in the US on Thursday, various media outlets reported.

The crash occurred near the New Jersey shoreline during a sightseeing tour, CNN reported Friday, citing law enforcement.

data.title: Asian Stocks Seesaw as U.S.-China Trade Tensions EscalateUpdate
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Asian equity markets see-sawed on Friday as volatility remained high, with traders weighing the intensifying U.S.-China trade conflict and the prospects for tariff deals.

Benchmark indexes in Japan and South Korea both ended lower after paring early losses.

Japan's Nikkei Stock Average lost 3.0%, dragged down by electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares.

South Korean auto stocks led the retreat on the Kospi exchange as President Trump's impending 25% tariff on autos continued to weigh on sentiment. South Korea's Kospi declined 0.5%.

Australia's S&P/ASX 200 closed 0.8% lower, also trimming early losses but still finishing lower in a roller-coaster week that featured its largest drop and largest gain since March 2020.

"Turbulence remains in play," Matt Simpson, market analyst at Forex.com and City Index, said in a commentary. "The excitement of a tariff pause has passed."

Earlier this week, Trump paused reciprocal tariffs on dozens of countries except for China, offering some relief but also generating uncertainty over how trade policy will play out. The White House said Thursday that the tariffs imposed on China by Trump in his second term add up to 145%, not the 125% it previously indicated.

Singapore's Straits Times Index was last down 1.7% while Malaysia's benchmark Kuala Lumpur Composite Index was off by 0.3%.

Shares in China, Hong Kong and Taiwan fared better, reversing early losses to swing into positive territory.

Taiwan's Taiex rose 2.8%, Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index gained 1.1% and China's Shanghai Composite Index added 0.45%.

Policymakers in the region continued to express concerns over the impact of U.S. tariffs on Asian economies and said they plan to hold trade negotiations to mitigate the tariffs' effects.

Japanese Economy Minister Ryosei Akazawa said Friday that he views the tariffs as a national crisis and that he will prioritize talks with the U.S. and make efforts to seek a positive outcome.

Fears of an escalating U.S.-China trade war appeared to prompt investors to seek shelter in havens like the Swiss franc, gold and government debt.

Yields on two-year Japanese government bonds fell 7.0 basis points to 0.605%, while those on two-year Australian sovereign debt slipped 5 basis points to 3.2560%. Yields on two-year New Zealand government securities dropped 5 basis points to 3.2510%.

Risk-off sentiment in financial markets also spurred appreciation of the yen against most other currencies. The U.S. dollar fell 0.7% to 143.39 yen, the Australian dollar dropped 0.9% to 89.07 yen and the Singapore dollar declined 0.4% to 108.18 yen.

Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com

data.title: Pirelli says it has no plans to invest in the United States
data.description:
Pirelli is not planning investments in the United States at the moment, the tyre maker said on Friday, contradicting a report in an Italian newspaper.

The Corriere della Sera said on Friday that Paolo Zampolli, U.S. President Donald Trump's special envoy for global partnerships, hoped that Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni would announce an investment by Pirelli during her visit to the White House on April 17.

He added that Pirelli would invest $1 billion in the U.S. state of Georgia to produce so-called smart tyres, potentially raising it to $2 billion in the future.

"At the moment nothing has been decided given the regulatory obstacles linked to questions of governance and shareholder structure," Pirelli said in a statement.

Pirelli executive vice chairman Marco Tronchetti Provera said this week that the company was having difficulties investing in the U.S. because its largest shareholder is China's state-owned Sinochem.

Pirelli's Chinese and Italian shareholders are at odds over the group's governance as Washington cracks down on Chinese technology in the automotive industry, banning key software and hardware from Chinese-controlled companies.

Trump has introduced a 25% tariff on auto imports, arguing this will encourage foreign carmakers and their suppliers to invest in the U.S. to avoid paying duties.

data.title: India's benchmarks set to erase weekly losses as US delays reciprocal tariffs
data.description:
** India's benchmark Nifty 50 and Sensex rise more than 2% each, set to erase week's losses

** U.S. delays hefty "retaliatory" tariffs beyond 10% by 90 days for most countries excluding China, providing reprieve for markets

** The pause on tariffs should provide modest upside for Indian equities, but uncertainties remain, says Kotak Institutional Equities

** Heavyweight financials , up 2.3%, the biggest contributors to benchmark's gains; HDFC Bank and Kotak Mahindra Bank rise 3% and 3.2%, respectively

** Metal stocks jump 4.8%, with JSW Steel , Tata Steel , and Hindalco Industries topping Nifty 50 gainers list, on weaker dollar and tariff reprieve

** All 13 major sectors in the green, while broader mid- and small-caps up 2% and 2.6%, respectively

** Muthoot Finance bucks trend, slips over 5% after Kotak Institutional Equities downgrades to "add" from "buy" following RBI's stricter norms for gold loan providers

data.title: U.K. Economy Shows Signs of Strength But Tariffs Darken Outlook3rd Update
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

The U.K. economy is growing more strongly than expected but U.S. President Trump's barrage of tariff hikes and pauses threaten to stymie further acceleration ahead.

Gross domestic product increased by 0.5% in February, figures from the Office for National Statistics showed Friday. That was a better performance than the marginal growth economists had expected, and means the U.K. economy is very likely to record growth over the first quarter as a whole, barring an unexpectedly large reversal in March.

February's growth was driven by strong output from Britain's factories, with manufacturing production unexpectedly rising 2.2% on month on the back of a rise in output from auto plants, as well as in electronics and pharmaceuticals, the ONS said.

The figures are "encouraging signs," government finance chief Rachel Reeves said.

"But we are not complacent. The world has changed," Reeves said in a post on X.

Despite the boost for the government's growth plans, the strength in manufacturing could be in part down to firms seeing a temporary rush of orders aiming to beat America's planned tariffs.

"It's possible the industrial sector is benefiting from tariff front-running," Capital Economics' Ruth Gregory said in a note.

"February's resilience is unlikely to last," she said, pointing to the wider global economic malaise stemming from uncertainty around Washington's trade policy.

After Trump last week unveiled a sweeping package of tariffs on goods imported into the U.S., London's FTSE 100 index suffered four straight days of losses, enduring on Monday its worst trading since the Covid-19 pandemic. Stocks rallied later in the week after Trump agreed to halt a central part of the tariff package amid the turmoil roiling markets at home and across the globe.

But a 10% tariff will remain in place on U.K. goods, as will a separate 25% duty on cars and metals. At more than 15% of the total, the U.S. is the largest export market for the U.K. goods, of which more than 58 billion pounds ($75.23 billion) worth crossed the Atlantic to America in the 12 months to last September, according to figures from the government's business department.

That makes the U.K. less exposed to tariffs than some neighbors, such as Germany, which sent 160 billion euros of goods to the U.S. last year, or Ireland, home to the operations of many American drugmakers. British goods exports also pale next to services exports to the U.S., worth some 124 billion pounds a year, and not liable to Trump's levies. But even a 10% tariff could knock Britain's already fragile economic growth.

And policymakers at the Bank of England could be forced into cutting interest rates more quickly than they had foreseen in a bid to lift some of the smog clouding activity in the country.

"The damaging aftereffects from this [tariff] turbulence will be felt for some time, with the resulting global uncertainty likely to further undermine the U.K. economy," said Suren Thiru, economics director at the Institute of Chartered Accountants in England and Wales. Businesses will also suffer from an increase in payroll costs from April, Thiru noted.

"The greater global financial and economic instability caused by the US tariff announcements makes a May rate cut look more likely than not, by further fuelling rate setters' concerns over the underlying resilience of the U.K. economy," Thiru said.

A more general trade war is likely to mean slower growth ahead for the U.K. economy Sustained high tariffs on British goods entering the U.S. market, as well as indirect effects from a chillier global trade landscape, could mean GDP expanding just 0.7% this year, compared with 1.5% in case tariffs were not implemented, according to calculations by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research, a think tank. Growth would be even feebler next year in that scenario, the NIESR said in a report.

"History shows how devastating a full-scale tariff war can be," NIESR economist Ahmet Kaya said.

The government last week said it would invite British businesses to shape the response to tariffs, and began compiling a list of American goods that could be targeted in retaliation.

"We are pressing the case for a trading relationship that supports businesses on both sides of the Atlantic," trade minister Jonathan Reynolds said.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer, meanwhile, this week said New York-based entertainment giant Comcast NBCUniversal would build a new theme park outside London, an investment he said would boost the British economy by billions of pounds.

"It will boost our world-class creative industries," he said. "It will drive growth across the country."

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0742 GMT - Unexpectedly rapid growth in the economy is good news for the U.K. government, but there is more to be done to ensure the country can ride out global turbulence, MHA's Joe Nellis says. Expansion of 0.5% in GDP in February was stronger than economists had pencilled in and leaves the U.K. firmly on track for a positive first quarter. That is likely in part down to higher government spending, Nellis says. But global instability is contributing to continued serious uncertainty around the economy, he says. "This is a good start, but much more will have to be done if the government are to reignite the economy," Nellis writes in a note.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0735 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise, tracking moves in U.S. Treasury yields and following stronger-than-expected U.K. gross domestic product data. The data are overshadowed by worsesning U.S.-China trade tensions after the U.S. imposed 145% tariffs on Chinese goods, causing a selloff in U.S. assets including Treasury bonds. "​President Trump's decision to impose a 145% tariff on Chinese imports has significantly disrupted global financial markets," IG analysts say in a note. U.K. annual GDP rose 1.4% in February, stronger than the 0.9% consensus forecast by economists in a WSJ poll. Ten-year gilt yields climb 6 basis points to 4.701%, Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0728 GMT - A sharp rise in British manufacturing output may be down to a tariff scramble and could prove short-lived, Deutsche Bank's chief U.K. economist, Sanjay Raja, writes in a note to clients. The economy grew unexpectedly strongly in February, expanding 0.5% on month, figures show Friday. A 2.2% increase in production of goods like cars, electronics and drugs helped drive that increase. But there are signs that the rise was down to firms stocking up their inventories ahead of the tariffs anticipate since President Trump's January inauguration, Raja says. "Some frontrunning of trade will have flattered the February (and very likely the March) data as well," he says. "Don't get too excited." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0721 GMT - Gold futures rise to a fresh record high as investors flock to safe-haven assets. Futures are up 1.6% at $3,226.90 a troy ounce, having climbed as high as $3,241.50/oz earlier in the session. The prior record of $3,201.60/oz was set on April 2, and was followed by a sharp selloff in the wake of U.S. President Trump's sweeping tariff announcements as investors liquidated holdings to cover losses elsewhere. The precious metal has since staged a strong rally, climbing 6.4% on-week. Uncertainty around tariff policies and escalating U.S.-China tensions have suppressed the U.S. dollar and increased recession risks, further supporting gold's safe-haven demand. Elsewhere, a softer-than-expected Consumer Price Index reading has raised hopes the Federal Reserve will increasingly ease monetary policy, another boon for bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0721 GMT - Sterling rises to its highest in a week against the dollar but stays weak against the euro after data showed the U.K. economy grew more strongly than expected in February, with gross domestic product up by 0.5%. Concerns about the impact of tariffs limit the currency's gains. "The economy grew much faster than expected in February," says Aberdeen economist Luke Bartholomew in a note, adding that the strength was broad. However, tariff developments will likely shape the outlook for the economy, he says. Sterling rises 0.6% to a high of $1.3057, according to FactSet, up from $1.3003 before the data. The euro rises 0.6% to 0.8690 pounds, however, having hit its highest in nearly 17 months at 0.8724 overnight, FactSet data show. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0720 GMT - The probability of stagflation in the U.S. is more likely than recession due to inflation shocks from tariffs, says Blerina Uruci, Chief U.S. Economist at T. Rowe Price. The extent to which inflation shock leads to sustained inflation depends largely on how it influences consumer and business inflation expectations. A rapid succession of these shocks increases the probability of stagflation, she says. Attempts by the current administration to excessively curb immigration could also lead to tighter labor markets and sustained inflationary pressures, she says. Supply shocks from tariffs and potential fiscal boosts could further exacerbate the supply-demand mismatch and increase the likelihood of stagflation, she adds.(monica.gupta@wsj.com)

0709 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi fell 0.5% to close at 2432.72, dragged by auto and battery stocks. The index lost 1.3% for the week. Foreign investors were net sellers in Friday's session after declines on Wall Street overnight. The U.S.-China trade tensions remain elevated following a clarification by the White House that U.S. tariffs on China total 145% and not 125%. Carmakers Hyundai Motor and Kia slid 5.1% and 7.0%, respectively, as 25% U.S. levies on car imports continued to weigh on sentiment. USD/KRW settled 0.4% lower at 1,449.90 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield was up 2.6 bps at 2.728%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0655 GMT - German Bunds and other eurozone government bond yields are little changed in early trading, outperforming U.S. Treasurys whose yields rise. "Earlier this week we noticed that the latest bout of risk-off has re-affirmed the international safe-haven status of German sovereign paper relative to U.S. Treasurys," Citi Research's Andrea Appeddu says in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is trading 0.4 basis point higher at 2.595%, while the 10-year Treasury yield is up 6.5 basis points, last trading at 4.458%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0652 GMT - Japanese stocks ended lower as growing U.S.-China trade tensions weighed on the global economic outlook. Electronics, pharmaceutical and auto shares led the losses. Sony Group dropped 7.4%, Chugai Pharmaceutical lost 6.4%, and Nissan Motor shed 6.3%. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 3.0% to 33585.58. Tariff-related headlines remain in focus. USD/JPY is at 144.13, down from 144.44 as of Thursday 5 p.m. Eastern time. The 2-year Japanese government bond yield dropped 7 bps to 0.605%, the 10-year yield fell 2.5 bps to 1.345% while the 20-year yield rose 6 bps at 2.365%. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

0641 GMT - The dollar slides, with the DXY dollar index dropping to its lowest in 21 months against a basket of currencies overnight, after the White House said Thursday that tariffs imposed on China add up to 145%, not the 125% previously indicated. "While this difference is negligible in any practical economic sense, the market reaction showed an increased sensitivity to the risks of a disorderly economic decoupling between the world's two largest economies," Deutsche Bank analysts say in a note. This overshadows previous relief about a delay in additional tariffs on countries except China. The DXY dollar index is last down 0.3% at 100.530, having hit a low of 99.705 overnight. The euro rises 0.6% to $1.1261, having hit a three-year high of 1.1373 overnight, according to FactSet. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0606 GMT - The recent volatility in the U.S. Treasury market has renewed speculation about what is driving the sell-off, say rate strategists at Societe Generale Research in a note. They point to foreign divestment and the unwinding of leveraged basis trades as two key forces being increasingly in focus. "Either or both appear to have likely contributed to strained liquidity conditions and amplifying market moves," the strategists say. They expect realized volatility in global bonds to remain high during the 90-day tariff pause as markets digest the impact of the higher tariff regime and recalibrate to incoming headlines. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0558 GMT - The Monetary Authority of Singapore is likely to ease its policy settings at its meeting Monday, according to all 10 economists and analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal. Singapore Prime Minister Wong's view of a "profound turning point" in global affairs and downside risks strike a clearly dovish tone, Citi Research's Wei Zheng Kit says, noting Wong's Ministerial Statement on April 8. There's a 65% probability for a measured 50bp reduction in the slope of the MAS' SGD nominal effective exchange rate policy band, the economist says in a research report. Unlike most central banks, the MAS' monetary policy framework is centered on managing SGD against trade-weighed basket of currencies. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Aeon: to Spend Y129.47B for Tender Offer
South Korea Jobless Rate Edges Up as Trade and Climate Pressures Loom
data.description:
South Korea's unemployment rate ticked up to 3.1% in March, a 0.1 percentage point increase from a year earlier, according to data from Statistics Korea released on Thursday, as labour market gains slowed and external risks mounted.

The number of unemployed rose by 26,000 to 918,000, while the tally of employed people increased by 193,000 to 28.59 million, lifting the employment-to-population ratio to 62.5%.

The labour force participation rate rose 0.3 percentage points to 64.6%, with the economically active population reaching 29.51 million.

The number of people not in the labour force dipped by 40,000 to 16.20 million.

Analysts at ING Research said domestic political uncertainty had eased following early April elections, but flagged downside risks from US tariffs and severe wildfires in the country's south.

"Tariffs and natural disasters are likely to weigh on labour markets going forward," ING said, adding that it expects the jobless rate to rise gradually in the coming months rather than lurch higher.

The United States imposed 25% tariffs on South Korean exports in March, a move that stung the country's trade-reliant economy and triggered a scramble for countermeasures in Seoul.

Officials are drafting support packages for sectors including autos.

Meanwhile, the southeast was ravaged by South Korea's worst wildfires on record, with at least 27 dead and more than 200 buildings destroyed.

The damage is likely to weigh on regional employment in the near term.

data.title: Lucid to Acquire Arizona Assets, Employees of Bankrupt Nikola
data.description:
Lucid said Friday it has agreed to acquire a number Arizona facilities and assets previously belonging to Nikola, subject to US bankruptcy court approval following an April 10.

Financial terms were not disclosed.

Lucid said it will take over a Nikola manufacturing facility and the Phoenix facility previously used as the bankrupt company's headquarters and product development center, adding more than 884,000 square feet to the company's Arizona footprint.

The company also said it plans to offer jobs in Arizona to more than 300 former Nikola employees.

The acquisitions do not include Nikola's business, customer base, or technology related to its hydrogen fuel cell electric trucks, the company said.

data.title: Chinese Shares Extend Winning Streak to Four Days Despite Escalating Tariffs
data.description:
Chinese shares erased earlier losses, ending Friday's session in the green and maintaining their winning streak, despite the US escalating the trade war with minimum tariffs on Chinese goods at 145%.

The Shanghai Composite Index, the main gauge of Chinese stocks, rose 0.5% to 3,238.23. The Shenzhen Component Index climbed 0.8% to 9,834.44. Both indices were up for a fourth straight session.

The White House on Thursday clarified that US President Donald Trump's announced 125% tariff increase was in addition to an existing 20% levy on Chinese goods related to fentanyl supply.

Elsewhere, China is reportedly seeking to strengthen its trading ties with the European Union as both sides expressed their willingness to resume a trade remedy dialogue amid the US' introduction of reciprocal tariffs.

Top European Union officials are expected to visit Beijing in late July for a summit with Chinese President Xi Jinping, the South China Morning Post reported. While no official date has been confirmed by China, the reported visit signals the EU's intent to strengthen ties with Beijing amid strained relations with the US.

In company news, Hunan Gold posted 2024 net profit attributable to shareholders of 846.5 million yuan, up 73% from 489.1 million yuan the previous year. Shares of the miner closed 5% higher Friday.

data.title: Oil Set for Weekly Loss Amid Trade War ConcernsMarket Talk
data.description: Oil prices rise in early trade on a softer U.S. dollar, but are still on course for a weekly loss as traders worry President Trump's trade war with China will weigh on global economic growth. Brent crude is up 1.4% at $64.18 a barrel, while WTI rises 1.4% to $60.93 a barrel. The dollar index--which measures the greenback against a half-dozen currencies--fell below 100, making commodities cheaper for holders of other currencies. Still, the crude benchmarks are poised to drop for a second straight week after Trump said tariffs on China add up to 145%, further raising concerns over a prolonged trade war between the world's two largest economies. "High level economic uncertainty is challenging for a macro-sensitive commodity such as oil," BMI analysts say. "We expect prices will remain under pressure as investors assess ongoing trade negotiations and rising tensions between Washington and Beijing." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0743 GMT - Oil prices rise in early trade on a softer U.S. dollar, but are still on course for a weekly loss as traders worry President Trump's trade war with China will weigh on global economic growth. Brent crude is up 1.4% at $64.18 a barrel, while WTI rises 1.4% to $60.93 a barrel. The dollar index--which measures the greenback against a half-dozen currencies--fell below 100, making commodities cheaper for holders of other currencies. Still, the crude benchmarks are poised to drop for a second straight week after Trump said tariffs on China add up to 145%, further raising concerns over a prolonged trade war between the world's two largest economies. "High level economic uncertainty is challenging for a macro-sensitive commodity such as oil," BMI analysts say. "We expect prices will remain under pressure as investors assess ongoing trade negotiations and rising tensions between Washington and Beijing." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0734 GMT - BP's first-quarter business update should result in a 10%-15% hit to consensus earnings expectations due to higher-than-expected quarterly tax rate, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo and Kai Ye Loh write. BP said Friday it expects its underlying effective tax rate to be around 50%. Investors are also unlikely to view the $4 billion increase in debt favorably, they write. Market watchers had expected a $2.2 billion increase in net debt, they add. The increases is largely due to an around $2 billon working capital increase, which should reverse in the following quarters and around $600 million from the Bunge acquisition, they write. Shares trade down 2.3% at 333.6 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0728 GMT - A sharp rise in British manufacturing output may be down to a tariff scramble and could prove short-lived, Deutsche Bank's chief U.K. economist, Sanjay Raja, writes in a note to clients. The economy grew unexpectedly strongly in February, expanding 0.5% on month, figures show Friday. A 2.2% increase in production of goods like cars, electronics and drugs helped drive that increase. But there are signs that the rise was down to firms stocking up their inventories ahead of the tariffs anticipate since President Trump's January inauguration, Raja says. "Some frontrunning of trade will have flattered the February (and very likely the March) data as well," he says. "Don't get too excited." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: Indian electronics products makers rise on government nod to PLI scheme
data.description:
** Dixon Technologies , Kaynes Technology and Amber Enterprises rise 7.5%, 5% and 5.8%, respectively

** Indian government approves production-linked incentive (PLI) scheme for electronics components, with a 229 bln-rupee ($2.66 bln) outlay

** Scheme offers turnover-linked, capex-linked and hybrid incentives

** JM Financial says DIXO, KAYN, AMBE likely to benefit from scheme

** YTD, DIXO, KAYN, AMBE down ~26%, ~35% and ~16%, respectively

($1 = 86.0200 Indian rupees)

S
